stem: Where should you mainly focus your eyes while driving on an open road?

choices.text: On the bumper of the car ahead
On the road markings just in front of your car
As far ahead as you can clearly see
On your speedometer

explanation.short: Look as far ahead as you can; this gives you the most time to plan.
explanation.examTip: Short-sighted looking causes late reactions and is a frequent test fault.

stem: How often should you glance at your interior mirror in normal driving?

choices.text: Only when braking
Roughly every 5 to 8 seconds and before every change of speed or direction
Only when you suspect someone is following you
Only on motorways

explanation.short: Glance at the mirror roughly every 5–8 seconds and before each speed or direction change.
explanation.examTip: Examiners specifically watch your mirror-use rhythm.

stem: You drive past a row of parked cars on a residential street. The greatest hidden hazard is:

choices.text: A police speed trap behind the cars
An oil patch on the road surface
A pedestrian or child stepping out between cars
A puncture from a piece of glass

explanation.short: A pedestrian or child emerging between parked cars is the classic hidden hazard.
explanation.examTip: Reduce speed and increase distance from the kerb when passing parked rows.

stem: How much sideways space should you give a cyclist when overtaking inside a built-up area?

choices.text: At least 1 metre
About 30 cm — the same as parking
Whatever the lane width allows
No gap is required if you sound the horn

explanation.short: Give cyclists at least 1 metre of side space — 1.5 m where speed is higher.
explanation.examTip: Cyclists may wobble or swerve around a parked car; expect it.

stem: You approach a school zone at the end of the school day. You should:

choices.text: Maintain speed because children are supervised
Reduce speed early and expect sudden movements off the kerb
Use the horn to warn children to keep back
Stop fully at every junction

explanation.short: Reduce speed early near schools; children may step out unexpectedly.
explanation.examTip: Anticipate before you see the hazard, not after.

stem: On a rural road with no pavement, on which side do pedestrians walk?

choices.text: On the right, with the traffic
On the left, facing oncoming traffic
Down the centre of the road
They are not allowed to walk on such roads

explanation.short: Pedestrians walk on the left, facing oncoming traffic, so they can see vehicles.
explanation.examTip: Dip headlights and keep well to your side of the road at dusk.

stem: When is a road typically most slippery after a dry spell?

choices.text: After several hours of heavy rain has washed it clean
During the first minutes of rain after a long dry period
Only when temperatures fall below freezing
Only on motorways

explanation.short: First rain after a dry spell lifts oil and dust to the surface, making the road most slippery.
explanation.examTip: Increase following distance and brake earlier as soon as drops start.

stem: When may you switch on your rear fog light?

choices.text: Whenever it is raining
Only when visibility is less than 50 metres
At any time on a motorway
Only between dusk and dawn

explanation.short: Rear fog lights are allowed only when visibility falls below 50 m.
explanation.examTip: Switch them off as soon as visibility improves — they dazzle the driver behind you.

stem: What does 'do not outdrive your headlights' mean?

choices.text: Always use full beam on unlit roads
Your stopping distance must fit inside the area you can see lit
Only drive at night with two headlights working
Avoid braking once you have switched on the headlights

explanation.short: Your stopping distance must fit inside the beam you can see; otherwise slow down.
explanation.examTip: On unlit rural roads this often means well below the legal limit.

stem: Roughly how long does an alert, healthy driver need to react to an unexpected hazard?

choices.text: About 0.1 second
About 1 second
About 3 seconds
Reaction is instant once you see the hazard

explanation.short: Alert drivers need roughly 1 second to react before braking even starts.
explanation.examTip: Tired, distracted or impaired drivers need noticeably longer.

image.alt: Diagram showing two cars with a 2-second following gap in dry conditions and a 4-second gap in wet conditions.
image.caption: The 2-second baseline gap in dry conditions, doubled in wet.

stem: In good conditions, what is the minimum recommended time-gap to the vehicle in front?

choices.text: 0.5 second
1 second
2 seconds
5 seconds

explanation.short: Keep at least a 2-second gap in good conditions.
explanation.examTip: Double the gap in rain; increase further in snow or fog.

stem: You see a ball roll into the road from between parked cars. The safest response is to:

choices.text: Continue at speed; balls do not damage cars
Brake sharply only if you see the child
Ease off, cover the brake, and be ready to stop for a child
Swerve into the oncoming lane

explanation.short: Treat a ball as a warning that a child may follow — ease off and cover the brake.
explanation.examTip: Anticipating like this means you almost never have to brake hard.

stem: How often should you glance in your mirrors while driving on an open road?

choices.text: Only before braking
Roughly every 5–8 seconds and before every manoeuvre
Only when overtaking
Once per minute is enough

explanation.short: Regular mirror checks every few seconds keep your picture of traffic behind you fresh.
explanation.examTip: Mirrors–signal–manoeuvre is the standard sequence examiners look for.

stem: You pass a primary school just before 8:30 in the morning. Several children are on the pavement. What should you do?

choices.text: Maintain speed and use the horn
Reduce speed and watch for children stepping into the road
Move to the centre of the road
Stop and wave them across

explanation.short: Children act unpredictably near schools; lower your speed and cover the brake.
explanation.examTip: The same care applies after school and around sport clubs and playgrounds.

stem: Visibility drops to about 50 metres in dense fog. Which lights should you use?

choices.text: High beam to see further
Dipped headlights and front fog lights; rear fog light is allowed
Hazard warning lights only
Side lights only

explanation.short: Use dipped beam (high beam reflects in fog), front fog lights as needed, and the rear fog light only when visibility is under 50 m.
explanation.examTip: Switch the rear fog off the moment visibility improves — it dazzles drivers behind you.

stem: You drive along a row of parked cars in a residential street. Which is the most useful clue that a car may be about to pull out?

choices.text: Brake lights or exhaust smoke
A clean windscreen
Empty roof racks
Number plate colour

explanation.short: Brake lights, exhaust fumes, front wheels turned outward and a driver in the seat all signal a possible pull-out.
explanation.examTip: Open doors and pedestrians arriving with keys are other early warnings.

stem: Inside town at 50 km/h you approach a parked ice-cream van on your right, with children visible nearby. A cyclist is following close behind you. What is the safest action?

choices.text: Keep your speed — the children are on the pavement, not the road
Slow well before the van and check the mirror so the cyclist sees you brake early
Sound your horn to warn the children
Swerve into the oncoming lane to give the van extra clearance

explanation.short: Children near a parked van are a high-risk hazard; slow early and warn the follower with brake lights, not the horn.
explanation.examTip: Anticipate the hidden pedestrian — do not wait until a child steps out.

stem: On a winter morning the dashboard shows +2 °C. You approach a bridge in a shaded valley. The road looks dry. What should you do?

choices.text: Maintain speed — +2 °C is above freezing, so no risk
Brake hard just before the bridge to test grip
Slow before the bridge and avoid sudden braking or steering on it
Use main beam to see the bridge surface

explanation.short: Bridges and shaded patches freeze first; +2 °C is well within the surface-ice risk window.
explanation.examTip: Treat shadowed tarmac and bridges in winter as potentially icy, even when the rest of the road looks dry.

stem: You are on a wet motorway following a lorry at 80 km/h with a 4-second gap. A car cuts into the gap between you and the lorry. What is the best response?

choices.text: Flash your headlights and accelerate to close the gap behind
Brake hard immediately to teach the driver a lesson
Ease off gently to reopen at least 4 seconds to the new car ahead
Overtake on the right to get past the car

explanation.short: Restore the gap by dropping back, not by braking hard or accelerating.
explanation.examTip: Tailgaters and cut-ins are managed by widening the gap in front of you — never by closing it.

stem: On a busy shopping street at 30 km/h you see: a traffic light in the distance turning amber, a bus pulling out of a stop ahead, a pedestrian at the kerb on your right, and a cyclist closing in your mirror. Which is the correct way to handle the picture?

choices.text: Focus only on the traffic light — it is the legal control
Treat all four as active hazards and adjust speed early
Ignore the cyclist behind — what is ahead matters more
Speed up to clear the bus before it pulls out

explanation.short: Active scanning means tracking far, mid, near and mirror at the same time; all four cues matter.
explanation.examTip: The CBR hazard-perception part rewards drivers who name multiple early cues, not just the most obvious one.

stem: You are driving past a row of parked cars on your right. Between two of them you notice a ball roll into the road. What is the safest anticipation?

choices.text: Ignore it — the ball alone is not a hazard
Brake firmly and expect a child to run out after it
Swerve into the oncoming lane to avoid the ball
Sound the horn and continue at speed

explanation.short: A ball in the road is a classic early cue that a child may follow — slow down sharply and be ready to stop.
explanation.examTip: Always read what is behind a visible cue, not just the cue itself.

stem: On a clear winter morning you are driving east and the sun is low and directly in your eyes, making it hard to see traffic lights and pedestrians ahead. What is the safest response?

choices.text: Maintain speed but squint harder
Slow down, use the sun visor and increase following distance
Switch on your full beam to compete with the sun
Stop in the road until the sun is higher

explanation.short: Reduced visibility means reduced speed and more space — combine the visor with a slower, more cautious pace.
explanation.examTip: Other drivers facing the sun may also miss signals; expect mistakes from oncoming and crossing traffic.

stem: Driving through a quiet residential area at 30 km/h you notice a car waiting at a side street on your right with its front wheels turned outward and the driver looking the other way. What is the best anticipation?

choices.text: You have priority — keep your speed
Ease off the accelerator and cover the brake in case the driver pulls out
Flash your headlights to claim priority
Speed up to pass before the driver looks back

explanation.short: Turned wheels and the driver's attention elsewhere are strong cues that the car may pull out — prepare to brake.
explanation.examTip: Legal priority is not safety — anticipate the mistake before it happens.

stem: An elderly pedestrian with a walking frame is halfway across a zebra crossing and stops to rest. What is the correct action?

choices.text: Sound your horn to encourage them to hurry
Wait patiently until they have fully cleared the crossing
Drive slowly around them
Continue once they are out of your lane

explanation.short: Pedestrians on a zebra crossing always keep priority; vulnerable users may need extra time.
explanation.examTip: Never pressure pedestrians by edging forward — give them the whole crossing.

stem: On a 50 km/h road you see the brake lights of several cars two or three vehicles ahead come on, even though the car directly in front of you has not braked yet. What should you do?

choices.text: Wait until the car in front brakes, then react
Ease off the throttle and prepare to brake now
Change lanes immediately without looking
Sound your horn to warn the cars ahead

explanation.short: Reading the traffic stream beyond the car in front gives you crucial extra seconds — start reacting before the driver ahead does.
explanation.examTip: Looking through the windows of the car in front is a CBR-recommended scanning habit.

stem: You are driving at 30 km/h along a residential street lined with parked cars. Between two parked vans on your right you notice a ball roll into the road. What is the safest response?

choices.text: Maintain speed; a ball cannot damage your car
Brake firmly and prepare for a child to follow the ball
Sound the horn and continue at the same speed
Swerve into the oncoming lane to give a wider berth

explanation.short: A ball into the road predicts a child running after it — slow firmly and be ready to stop.
explanation.examTip: CBR scenarios use the ball-then-child pattern often; the correct answer is always to brake.

stem: You are about to turn right at a junction. A cyclist on the cycle path to your right is travelling straight ahead at the same speed as you. What must you do?

choices.text: Turn first, because cars have priority over cyclists
Stop and let the cyclist go straight on before you turn
Speed up to clear the turn before the cyclist arrives
Sound the horn to make the cyclist slow down

explanation.short: A straight-going cyclist on a parallel cycle path has priority when you turn right across their line.
explanation.examTip: Always do a final blind-spot check over the right shoulder before turning right across a cycle path.

stem: You are driving on a rural road. Visibility drops in patchy fog to about 80 metres. Which lights may you use?

choices.text: Main beam, to see further into the fog
Dipped headlights and front fog lights; rear fog lights are not yet allowed
Dipped headlights, plus front and rear fog lights
Hazard lights only

explanation.short: Below 200 m you may use front fog lights; rear fog lights are only allowed below 50 m.
explanation.examTip: Main beam in fog reflects off the droplets and dazzles you — never use it.

stem: On a 100 km/h motorway in the rain, a driver behind you sits very close to your rear bumper. What is the safest action?

choices.text: Brake firmly to teach them a lesson
Speed up to escape them
Ease off gently to enlarge the gap in front, so any braking can be gradual
Switch on rear fog lights to dazzle them

explanation.short: Open extra space ahead so you never need a hard brake — the tailgater needs the longer gap, not you.
explanation.examTip: In wet conditions extend the 2-second rule to 4 seconds; a tailgater means you need even more.

stem: You drive at 50 km/h on a busy shopping street. The traffic light 150 metres ahead has just turned green, but you see brake lights from cars between you and the light. Where should you mainly look?

choices.text: Only at the green light, because that determines whether you can continue
Mainly at the brake lights, scanning further ahead for the reason behind them
At the pavement on your right, to spot pedestrians
At the rear-view mirror to plan a lane change

explanation.short: Brake lights between you and a green light signal a hidden hazard — look past them to find the cause and react early.
explanation.examTip: A green light only gives permission, never an obligation to drive forward; always read the cars in front first.

stem: On a 60 km/h rural road you approach an elderly cyclist riding slightly into the carriageway. The road bends right ahead and you cannot see oncoming traffic. What is the safest action?

choices.text: Overtake at once before reaching the bend
Sound the horn and squeeze past, keeping close to the cyclist to leave room
Drop back, match the cyclist's speed, and wait until the road opens up and you can give 1.5 m of clearance
Flash your headlights so the cyclist moves further onto the verge

explanation.short: Outside built-up areas you should leave at least 1.5 m when passing a cyclist — never overtake near a blind bend.
explanation.examTip: Elderly cyclists can wobble or veer unexpectedly; assume they will not hold a perfectly straight line.

stem: At night on an unlit rural road, an oncoming driver leaves their high beams on and you are dazzled. What should you do?

choices.text: Flash your own high beams back to teach them a lesson
Look towards the right edge of your lane and slow down until your eyes recover
Close your eyes briefly to reset
Speed up so you pass the oncoming car faster

explanation.short: Shift your gaze to the right kerb or lane marking and reduce speed — looking away from the headlights protects your night vision.
explanation.examTip: Flashing high beams back blinds two drivers instead of one. Reduce speed and hold the lane.

stem: On a 100 km/h motorway you suddenly see a long stationary queue 200 metres ahead. There is no shoulder on your right and a lorry is close behind you. What should you do?

choices.text: Brake as hard as possible to stop as far from the queue as you can
Switch on hazard lights, brake firmly but progressively, and stop with extra space behind the last car
Steer onto the right verge so the lorry has more space
Maintain speed and change lane left without checking

explanation.short: Hazard lights warn the lorry behind, progressive braking avoids a rear-end shunt, and extra buffer space gives you somewhere to go if hit from behind.
explanation.examTip: Switching on hazards at the back of a sudden queue on a motorway is standard Dutch practice — it warns everyone behind, not just the next car.

stem: Driving at 30 km/h on a residential street, you see a football roll across the road from between parked cars on your right. No child is yet visible. What should you do?

choices.text: Continue at 30 km/h — the ball is no danger to your car
Cover the brake, reduce speed sharply, and expect a child to follow the ball
Swerve into the oncoming lane to miss the ball
Sound the horn to scare any child away from the road

explanation.short: A ball on the road is a strong clue that a child may follow without looking — cover the brake and slow down at once.
explanation.examTip: Always treat unattended balls, scooters, or pet toys near parked cars as a warning of a hidden child.

stem: After a hot dry week it begins to rain heavily on a motorway. In which window is the road surface usually most slippery?

choices.text: Once the rain has been falling for over an hour
In the first 10 to 15 minutes, before rubber and oil are washed away
Only after the rain stops
Only at temperatures below 5 °C

explanation.short: The first 10–15 minutes of rain after a dry spell mix water with built-up oil and rubber on the surface, creating the most slippery conditions.
explanation.examTip: Expect reduced grip and increase your following distance the moment it starts raining, not after the storm has settled.
